title: Restraint Safety and Patient Rights in a 68-Year-Old Man with Hyperactive Delirium Pulling at His IV and Oxygen and Climbing Over the Bed Rails: Treating the Underlying Cause, Exhausting Least-Restrictive Alternatives First, and—Only When Necessary—Applying the Correct Restraint with a Proper (Non-PRN) Order, Time Limits, Continuous Monitoring, Safe Application, and Earliest Possible Discontinuation
subtitle: RESTRAINTS = any manual method, physical/mechanical device, material, or equipment that immobilizes or reduces a patient's ability to move freely, OR a drug used to manage behavior/restrict movement that is NOT a standard treatment/dose for the patient's condition (CHEMICAL restraint). SECLUSION = involuntary confinement alone in a room the patient cannot leave. CORE ETHICAL/LEGAL PRINCIPLES (CMS Conditions of Participation + Joint Commission + state law): restraints are a LAST RESORT used ONLY to ensure the immediate physical safety of the patient/staff/others when LESS-RESTRICTIVE interventions have failed; they must NEVER be used for staff convenience, discipline, punishment, coercion, or retaliation; they must be the LEAST RESTRICTIVE effective intervention, for the SHORTEST time, discontinued at the EARLIEST possible moment; and the patient retains rights to safe, dignified, respectful care. TWO INDICATION CATEGORIES with DIFFERENT rules: (1) NON-VIOLENT / NON-SELF-DESTRUCTIVE ('medical-surgical') restraint — to support medical healing/protect medical devices when a patient interferes with essential treatment (e.g., pulling at an endotracheal tube, central line, surgical drain) — orders renewed per facility policy (commonly each calendar day) with regular reassessment; (2) VIOLENT / SELF-DESTRUCTIVE ('behavioral') restraint — to protect the patient or others from IMMINENT harm from violent/aggressive behavior — stricter rules: a face-to-face evaluation by a licensed independent practitioner (or trained RN per policy) within 1 HOUR of initiation, and TIME-LIMITED orders: ADULTS ≥18 yr up to 4 hours, ages 9-17 up to 2 hours, under 9 up to 1 hour (renewable within those limits up to 24 hours, then a new order/exam). ORDER RULES (both types): a restraint requires a PROVIDER ORDER based on an individualized assessment; orders may NEVER be written as 'PRN'/standing/as-needed; in an EMERGENCY the RN MAY INITIATE restraint to protect immediate safety and obtain the order as soon as possible (per policy, typically within minutes/1 hour). LEAST-RESTRICTIVE ALTERNATIVES — must be tried/considered FIRST and continued alongside any restraint: identify + TREAT THE UNDERLYING CAUSE (in delirium: hypoxia, pain, infection/sepsis, urinary retention/full bladder, constipation, electrolyte/glucose derangement, medication effects/withdrawal, sleep deprivation); REORIENTATION + calm reassurance + familiar objects + glasses/hearing aids; FAMILY/sitter presence or a 1:1 observer; environmental measures (LOW bed, bed/chair ALARM, frequent rounding/toileting, clutter-free, adequate lighting, reduce tethers — e.g., intermittent rather than continuous lines, discontinue unnecessary catheters/telemetry); diversion/activity; address hunger/thirst; de-escalation techniques for agitation. SAFE APPLICATION (if restraint is used): use the correct, properly sized device; secure ties to the MOVABLE BED FRAME (NOT the side rails) with QUICK-RELEASE knots; ensure 2 fingers fit under limb restraints (circulation); position to protect the AIRWAY (avoid supine flat if aspiration risk; HOB elevated) and never restrain in a way that compromises breathing; pad bony prominences; keep a call light/release within reach when appropriate. NOTE on SIDE RAILS: raising ALL FOUR side rails to prevent a patient from getting out of bed is considered a RESTRAINT (raising rails to assist mobility/positioning, or for a patient on a stretcher, is not). MONITORING + CARE while restrained (RN assessment; some tasks delegated to UAP with RN supervision): regular checks (VIOLENT/behavioral restraints require CONTINUOUS monitoring; non-violent per policy, e.g., every 1-2 hours) of NEUROVASCULAR/circulation status, skin integrity, correct application, vital signs, and provide RANGE OF MOTION, repositioning, hydration/nutrition, TOILETING, hygiene, and emotional support/comfort at regular intervals; assess behavior + readiness for discontinuation frequently and RELEASE as soon as safety criteria are met (trial release of one limb, etc.). COMPLICATIONS of restraints (why they are last resort): physical injury, strangulation/ASPHYXIATION/death, aspiration, pressure injuries, neurovascular compromise, incontinence, deconditioning/functional decline, WORSENED agitation/delirium, and psychological trauma — restraints can paradoxically INCREASE the harm they aim to prevent. DOCUMENTATION: the behavior necessitating restraint, the less-restrictive measures attempted and their failure, the order, type/site, times applied/renewed/released, monitoring + care provided, the patient's response, and family notification; conduct DEBRIEFING (especially after behavioral restraint/seclusion) to learn and reduce future use. DELEGATION: ASSESSMENT, the decision to restrain, and evaluation remain the RN's responsibility; routine monitoring/care tasks (vital signs, food/fluids, toileting, ROM, repositioning, observation/sitting) may be delegated to UAP per scope with RN supervision and clear instructions. The aim throughout: PROTECT SAFETY while preserving DIGNITY and rights, minimize/avoid restraint, and remove it at the first safe opportunity

patient.name: H. Petrov
patient.code: Full code; baseline decisional but currently has FLUCTUATING capacity due to acute delirium; wife is healthcare proxy + present; goals = keep him safe, find + treat the cause of his confusion, avoid harm, and avoid restraints if at all possible
patient.admitDate: Hospital day 3 for community-acquired pneumonia + a urinary tract infection with new HYPERACTIVE DELIRIUM (acute, fluctuating confusion with agitation) superimposed on no prior dementia. Tonight he is pulling at his peripheral IV and nasal-cannula oxygen, repeatedly trying to climb OVER the bed rails, and intermittently swatting at staff—creating a high risk of a fall, injury, and removal of needed treatment. The team is working up + treating delirium causes while the nurse implements least-restrictive measures and evaluates whether a restraint is temporarily necessary for safety.
patient.room: Medical unit; bed in low position with a bed alarm; wife at bedside; sitter being arranged; multidisciplinary team (hospitalist, nurse, pharmacist, PT/OT) addressing delirium causes; quiet, well-lit environment being optimized

vitals.spo2: 89% on room air (had pulled off his oxygen) → 94% once nasal cannula replaced
vitals.temp: 38.3°C; acutely agitated + disoriented + pulling at IV/oxygen + climbing over the side rails; not oriented to place/time; intermittently combative when redirected; this is a CHANGE from his calmer daytime status (delirium fluctuates)

38.3°C; rapid assessment for delirium contributors: HYPOXIA (corrected with O2 replacement + improved SpO2), pain (grimacing — assessed/treated), FULL BLADDER (bladder scan → 520 mL, toileted/straight-cathed per orders → relief + calmer), infection/fever (antipyretic + ongoing antibiotics for PNA/UTI), reviewed meds for deliriogenic/anticholinergic agents, checked glucose + electrolytes; wife brought his glasses + hearing aid + reoriented; environment quieted

37.9°C; with O2 restored, bladder emptied, pain treated, reorientation, glasses/hearing aid, wife presence, low bed + bed alarm, frequent rounding, and a 1:1 sitter arranged, he is calmer but still intermittently reaching for his IV (which is delivering needed antibiotics) and unsteady/impulsive (fall risk); team weighing whether a temporary least-restrictive device is needed for safety while causes resolve

vitals.time: 2300 (brief restraint use after alternatives, with proper order)
vitals.temp: 37.7°C; because he continued to pull at the IV delivering essential antibiotics despite alternatives + a sitter, the LEAST-RESTRICTIVE effective device (soft mitts / a single soft limb restraint to the affected arm) was applied as a NON-VIOLENT/medical-surgical restraint to protect treatment; provider order obtained (not PRN; individualized); ties secured to the bed FRAME with quick-release knots, 2 fingers' clearance, HOB elevated; monitoring + ROM + circulation checks + toileting + reassessment scheduled; sitter + wife remain; plan to release at the earliest safe moment

37.2°C; delirium clearing as pneumonia/UTI treated, oxygenation maintained, bladder managed, and sleep-wake/orientation supported; he is calmer, oriented to person/place, no longer pulling at lines; the restraint was DISCONTINUED at the earliest safe opportunity after a successful trial release; skin/neurovascular intact; debrief + documentation completed; continuing nonpharmacologic delirium prevention

36.8°C; delirium resolved with treatment of the underlying infection + supportive measures; mentation back to baseline per wife; ambulating with PT; no restraint in use; no injury; reoriented + comfortable; planning discharge with delirium-prevention education for the family

labs.n: PRESENTATION + the safety problem + delirium context
labs.v: 68 y/o man H. Petrov, hospital day 3 for community-acquired PNEUMONIA + a urinary tract infection, with NEW HYPERACTIVE DELIRIUM (acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, disorganized thinking, altered consciousness) superimposed on NO prior dementia. Tonight he became acutely agitated — PULLING OFF his nasal-cannula oxygen (SpO2 dropped to 89%), PULLING AT his peripheral IV (delivering essential antibiotics), repeatedly trying to CLIMB OVER the bed rails, and intermittently SWATTING at staff when redirected — creating a high risk of a FALL, injury, and removal of needed treatment. This is a marked change from his calmer daytime status (delirium characteristically fluctuates, often worsening at night/'sundowning'). PMH: hypertension, BPH, mild hearing loss, osteoarthritis; no dementia; independent at baseline (lives with wife, gardens, drives). MEDICATIONS on admission + a few added in hospital (being reviewed for deliriogenic/anticholinergic agents + a sleep aid). SOCIAL: married 40 years; wife is healthcare proxy + present at the bedside, distressed to see him 'not himself'; two adult children; retired machinist; values independence + dignity. The clinical + ethical task: keep him and staff SAFE right now, urgently identify + TREAT the reversible causes of his delirium, implement LEAST-RESTRICTIVE measures, and use a restraint ONLY if necessary for immediate safety — correctly, briefly, and with full respect for his rights and dignity. The nurse leads the bedside response, mobilizes a sitter + the wife, and coordinates with the provider.
labs.r: Acute hyperactive delirium with behaviors endangering safety (hypoxia from pulling O2, fall risk from climbing rails, removal of essential IV antibiotics, combativeness). The priorities are immediate safety + urgent treatment of reversible delirium causes + least-restrictive measures, reserving restraint as a last resort. RN role: stabilize safety, find/treat causes, mobilize alternatives, protect rights.

labs.n: Least-restrictive alternatives + treating the cause FIRST
labs.v: LEAST-RESTRICTIVE ALTERNATIVES must be implemented FIRST (and continued even if a restraint becomes necessary). The single highest-yield intervention in delirium is to IDENTIFY + TREAT THE UNDERLYING CAUSE — use a mnemonic-style scan for reversible contributors: HYPOXIA (this patient pulled off O2 → SpO2 89% → replace O2, which alone often calms agitation), PAIN (assess + treat — untreated pain is a common, fixable driver), INFECTION/SEPSIS (his PNA/UTI — continue antibiotics + antipyretic), URINARY RETENTION / FULL BLADDER (bladder scan → 520 mL → toilet/catheterize → relief), CONSTIPATION/impaction, ELECTROLYTE/GLUCOSE derangements (check Na, Ca, glucose), MEDICATIONS (review for deliriogenic/ANTICHOLINERGIC agents, benzodiazepines, opioids, recently added sleep aids; deprescribe offenders), ALCOHOL/SEDATIVE WITHDRAWAL, SLEEP DEPRIVATION, and sensory deprivation. NON-PHARMACOLOGIC + ENVIRONMENTAL measures: REORIENT calmly + repeatedly (clock, calendar, explain where he is + why); provide his GLASSES + HEARING AID (sensory correction dramatically reduces confusion); FAMILY presence (his wife) + a SITTER / 1:1 observer; LOW bed + BED/CHAIR ALARM + frequent rounding + scheduled TOILETING; reduce 'tethers' (convert continuous IV to intermittent/saline-lock when possible, remove unnecessary catheters/telemetry/lines, hide/secure tubing); quiet, well-lit room, day-night cues, minimize nighttime interruptions to support sleep; de-escalation (calm voice, simple choices, avoid arguing/confrontation, validate feelings); diversion/comfort; address hunger/thirst. PHARMACOLOGIC management of delirium is itself a careful, limited tool: treat the cause; use antipsychotics only for severe agitation posing a safety risk that is unresponsive to nonpharmacologic measures (low dose, short term), and AVOID benzodiazepines (they worsen delirium — except in alcohol/sedative withdrawal). These measures frequently obviate the need for any physical restraint; here, treating hypoxia + a full bladder + pain + reorientation + a sitter substantially calmed him. ONLY IF less-restrictive measures FAIL and the patient remains an imminent danger to safety (here, continuing to pull the IV delivering essential antibiotics + fall risk) is a restraint considered — as the least restrictive option that works, for the shortest time.
labs.r: Try/maintain least-restrictive alternatives FIRST: above all TREAT THE CAUSE of delirium (hypoxia, pain, infection, full bladder, meds, electrolytes, sleep) + reorient + glasses/hearing aid + family/sitter + low bed/alarm + reduce tethers + de-escalation; avoid benzodiazepines. Restraint is considered only when these fail and imminent danger persists. RN role: cause-directed + nonpharmacologic measures before any restraint.

labs.n: Restraint types, order rules, and time limits
labs.v: If a restraint becomes necessary, the TYPE + the RULES depend on the indication. RESTRAINT TYPES: PHYSICAL/mechanical — soft limb restraints, mitts (prevent pulling lines while leaving wrists free), vest/jacket, lap/belt; CHEMICAL — a medication used to control behavior or restrict movement that is NOT a standard treatment/dose for the patient's condition (using a drug as a restraint triggers restraint rules); SECLUSION — involuntary confinement alone. SIDE RAILS: raising ALL FOUR to prevent a patient from exiting the bed counts as a RESTRAINT (whereas raising rails to aid repositioning/mobility, or two rails for a stretcher, does not). INDICATION CATEGORIES + RULES: (A) NON-VIOLENT / NON-SELF-DESTRUCTIVE ('medical-surgical') — used to protect essential medical treatment/devices when the patient interferes (this patient pulling the IV delivering antibiotics; preventing ETT/line/drain removal) → requires a provider order based on individual assessment, renewed per facility policy (commonly each calendar day) with ongoing reassessment; monitoring per policy (e.g., every 1-2 hours). (B) VIOLENT / SELF-DESTRUCTIVE ('behavioral') — used when violent/aggressive behavior poses IMMINENT danger to the patient/others → STRICTER: a FACE-TO-FACE EVALUATION by a licensed independent practitioner (or trained RN per policy) within 1 HOUR of initiation, and TIME-LIMITED orders: ADULT (≥18) up to 4 HOURS, ages 9-17 up to 2 HOURS, under 9 up to 1 HOUR, renewable within those limits up to 24 hours, then a new order + exam; CONTINUOUS monitoring. UNIVERSAL ORDER RULES (both types): a restraint ALWAYS requires a PROVIDER ORDER based on an individualized assessment; orders can NEVER be 'PRN'/standing/as-needed (each episode needs its own order); the order specifies the type, reason, and duration; in an EMERGENCY the RN MAY INITIATE a restraint to protect immediate safety and must obtain the order as soon as possible (per policy). Restraints are NEVER ordered or used for staff convenience, discipline, punishment, coercion, or as a substitute for adequate staffing/monitoring. THIS PATIENT: his behavior is primarily a NON-VIOLENT/medical-surgical situation (protecting the IV antibiotics + safety) rather than purposeful violence; the least-restrictive effective device (soft mitts or a single soft limb restraint to the affected arm) is selected, with a proper individualized (non-PRN) order, after alternatives + a sitter were insufficient.
labs.r: Restraint types: physical (soft limb/mitts/vest/belt), chemical (drug to control behavior, not standard treatment), seclusion; 4 side rails up = restraint. NON-VIOLENT/medical (protect treatment/devices) → order renewed per policy (often daily). VIOLENT/behavioral (imminent harm) → 1-hr face-to-face eval + time limits (adult ≤4h, 9-17 ≤2h, <9 ≤1h). ALWAYS a provider order, NEVER PRN; RN may initiate in an emergency + get the order ASAP; never for convenience/punishment. RN role: choose least-restrictive type + ensure proper order/rules.

labs.v: SAFE APPLICATION (if a physical restraint is used): select the CORRECT, properly sized device and the LEAST restrictive type that achieves safety (e.g., soft MITTS that prevent pulling lines while leaving the wrists free, or a single soft limb restraint, before anything more restrictive); secure the ties to the MOVABLE BED FRAME — NEVER to the side rails (a rail movement could injure the patient) — using QUICK-RELEASE knots that can be undone instantly in an emergency; ensure TWO FINGERS fit under a limb restraint to preserve circulation; pad bony prominences; POSITION to protect the AIRWAY and prevent aspiration (elevate the head of the bed; avoid restraining a patient flat/supine in a way that risks aspiration; NEVER apply anything that restricts breathing or chest expansion); keep the call light + a means of summoning help within reach when appropriate; remove eyeglasses-entangling or strangulation hazards. MONITORING + CARE while restrained (RN performs/oversees assessment; UAP may perform delegated care with supervision): for VIOLENT/behavioral restraints, CONTINUOUS monitoring; for NON-VIOLENT, per policy (commonly every 1-2 hours) — assess NEUROVASCULAR/CIRCULATION (color, temperature, pulses, capillary refill, sensation/movement distal to the restraint), SKIN integrity at the restraint site, correct/safe application, vital signs, hydration/nutrition, and behavior. At regular intervals (e.g., at least every 2 hours) provide RANGE OF MOTION, repositioning, offer FLUIDS/nutrition, TOILETING, hygiene, and emotional support/reassurance — releasing one limb at a time as needed. Continuously reassess the NEED + the patient's READINESS for discontinuation, and DISCONTINUE at the EARLIEST safe opportunity (do a TRIAL release as criteria are met — calmer, oriented, no longer endangering self/treatment) rather than waiting for an order to expire. DOCUMENT thoroughly: the behavior necessitating restraint, the less-restrictive measures tried + their failure, the order, the type/site/times applied-renewed-released, all monitoring + care provided, the patient's response, injuries (if any), and family notification. Conduct a DEBRIEFING (especially after behavioral restraint/seclusion) with the team (and patient/family when appropriate) to learn and reduce future restraint use. THIS PATIENT: soft mitts/single soft limb restraint applied to the bed frame with quick-release knots + 2-finger clearance + HOB elevated; q1-2h neurovascular/skin/ROM/toileting checks + sitter + wife present; restraint DISCONTINUED the next morning after a successful trial release as his delirium cleared—no injury, skin/circulation intact.
labs.r: Safe application: least-restrictive correct/sized device, tie to the BED FRAME (not rails) with QUICK-RELEASE knots, 2-finger clearance, HOB up to protect airway, never restrict breathing. Monitor (continuous for behavioral; ~q1-2h for non-violent) circulation/skin/application/VS + provide ROM, fluids, toileting, repositioning, reassurance ~q2h; reassess + DISCONTINUE at earliest safe moment (trial release). Document fully + debrief. RN role: safe application + monitoring + timely removal.

labs.n: Patient rights, complications, delegation; Pertinent NEGATIVES
labs.v: PATIENT RIGHTS + DIGNITY: a patient has the right to be FREE FROM restraint/seclusion imposed for staff convenience, discipline, coercion, punishment, or retaliation, or as a substitute for adequate monitoring/staffing; restraint may be used ONLY to protect immediate physical safety, as a LAST RESORT, with the LEAST restrictive method, for the SHORTEST duration; the patient/surrogate should be informed and the FAMILY notified per policy; care must preserve dignity (privacy, explanation, reassurance). COMPLICATIONS (why restraints are last resort + must be minimized): physical injury/bruising, STRANGULATION/ASPHYXIATION and death (improper application/airway compromise), ASPIRATION, PRESSURE INJURIES, neurovascular compromise/nerve injury, incontinence + skin breakdown, deconditioning + functional decline, WORSENED agitation/delirium + psychological trauma/fear — restraints can paradoxically increase the very harms they aim to prevent, so the safest restraint is often NO restraint plus a sitter + cause-directed treatment. DELEGATION + INTERPROFESSIONAL: the ASSESSMENT, the decision to restrain, the evaluation of response, and the decision to discontinue are the REGISTERED NURSE's responsibility and CANNOT be delegated; routine MONITORING/CARE tasks during restraint (taking vital signs, offering food/fluids, toileting, assisting ROM/repositioning, sitting/observing as a 1:1) may be DELEGATED to unlicensed assistive personnel within their scope, with clear instructions and RN SUPERVISION; the provider order + face-to-face evaluation (behavioral) involve the LIP; pharmacy + the team address deliriogenic medications. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice in this case): the restraint was NOT used for convenience/punishment; NOT applied before trying least-restrictive measures; NOT ordered PRN; NOT tied to the side rails; NOT left without monitoring; and the underlying delirium cause was treated rather than merely 'restraining the symptom'; no injury, aspiration, or neurovascular compromise occurred; benzodiazepines (which worsen delirium) were avoided. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: prompt recognition + treatment of reversible delirium causes (hypoxia, full bladder, pain, infection); engaged wife + a sitter; least-restrictive approach with a proper order, safe application, vigilant monitoring, and EARLY discontinuation; reversible (non-dementia) delirium with a treatable infection; thorough documentation + debriefing + family education for prevention. EXPECTED: delirium resolves with treatment, the restraint is brief, no injury occurs, dignity is preserved, and the patient returns to baseline. The case models the balance: PROTECT SAFETY while MINIMIZING restraint + honoring rights.
labs.r: Rights: free from restraint for convenience/punishment; last resort, least restrictive, shortest time, family notified, dignity preserved. Complications (injury, asphyxiation/death, aspiration, pressure injury, neurovascular, worsened delirium) make minimization essential. RN owns assessment/decision/discontinuation; UAP may do delegated monitoring/care with supervision. Negatives confirm correct practice (not for convenience, alternatives first, not PRN, tied to frame, monitored, cause treated). RN role: protect safety + rights + minimize restraint.

nursesNote: Night 2200 — 68 y/o man H. Petrov, hospital day 3 for community-acquired pneumonia + UTI, with NEW HYPERACTIVE DELIRIUM (acute, fluctuating; no prior dementia). Acutely agitated: PULLED OFF nasal-cannula O2 (SpO2 dropped to 89%), PULLING at peripheral IV delivering antibiotics, repeatedly CLIMBING over the side rails, intermittently SWATTING at staff — high risk of fall, injury, and loss of essential treatment; marked change from calmer daytime status (delirium fluctuates/worse at night). PMH: HTN, BPH, mild hearing loss, OA; independent at baseline; wife (healthcare proxy) at bedside, distressed. PRIORITIES: immediate safety + urgent treatment of reversible delirium causes + LEAST-RESTRICTIVE measures, restraint only as last resort. 2210 — assessed/treated delirium contributors: HYPOXIA (replaced O2 → SpO2 94%, which itself calmed him), PAIN (assessed/treated), FULL BLADDER (bladder scan 520 mL → toileted/cathed per orders → relief + calmer), infection/fever (antipyretic + continued antibiotics), reviewed meds for deliriogenic/anticholinergic agents + recently added sleep aid, checked glucose/electrolytes; wife brought GLASSES + HEARING AID + reoriented; quieted/lit environment; AVOIDED benzodiazepines (worsen delirium). 2240 — least-restrictive bundle in place: low bed + bed alarm + frequent rounding + scheduled toileting + reduced tethers (saline-locked between antibiotic doses where possible) + 1:1 SITTER arranged + wife presence; calmer but still intermittently reaching for the IV (essential antibiotics) + impulsive/unsteady (fall risk). 2300 — because he continued pulling at the IV despite alternatives + sitter, applied the LEAST-RESTRICTIVE effective device (soft MITTS / single soft limb restraint to that arm) as a NON-VIOLENT/medical-surgical restraint to protect treatment; obtained an individualized PROVIDER ORDER (NOT PRN); ties secured to the BED FRAME (not side rails) with QUICK-RELEASE knots, 2-finger clearance, HOB elevated to protect airway; q1-2h neurovascular/circulation + skin + application checks + ROM + fluids + toileting + reassurance; sitter + wife remain; plan to release at the earliest safe moment; documented behavior, alternatives tried + failed, order, type/site/time, monitoring, response, and family notification. Day 4 AM — delirium clearing as PNA/UTI treated + oxygenation maintained + bladder managed + sleep/orientation supported; calmer, oriented to person/place, no longer pulling lines → restraint DISCONTINUED at the earliest safe opportunity after a successful trial release; skin/neurovascular intact, no injury; debrief + documentation completed; continuing nonpharmacologic delirium-prevention bundle. Day 6 — delirium RESOLVED; mentation at baseline per wife; ambulating with PT; no restraint; no injury; discharge planning with family delirium-prevention education. RIGHTS/dignity preserved throughout: restraint not used for convenience/punishment, alternatives first, not PRN, tied to frame, continuously cared for, cause treated, removed early. ASSESSMENT/decision/discontinuation were RN responsibilities; monitoring/care tasks (VS, fluids, toileting, ROM, sitting) delegated to UAP with supervision.
providerOrders: ADMIT/CONTINUE: medical unit; hospital day 3 — pneumonia + UTI with new hyperactive delirium; fall + safety precautions
DIAGNOSIS: acute hyperactive delirium (multifactorial) superimposed on baseline normal cognition, with behaviors endangering safety/treatment; restraint considered as last resort
CODE STATUS: full code; fluctuating capacity (delirium); wife = healthcare proxy
TREAT REVERSIBLE DELIRIUM CAUSES (priority):
Maintain oxygenation (replace/secure O2; treat hypoxia); continue antibiotics for PNA/UTI; antipyretic for fever
Assess + treat pain; bladder scan + relieve urinary retention; assess for constipation
Check glucose + electrolytes (Na/Ca) + correct; review/deprescribe deliriogenic + anticholinergic meds + sleep aids
AVOID benzodiazepines (worsen delirium) except for alcohol/sedative withdrawal
LEAST-RESTRICTIVE ALTERNATIVES FIRST (and continue):
Reorientation; provide glasses + hearing aid; calm de-escalation; family presence; 1:1 sitter/observer
Low bed + bed/chair alarm; frequent rounding + scheduled toileting; reduce tethers (saline-lock IV between doses, remove unnecessary lines/catheters/telemetry)
Quiet, well-lit room; day-night cues; protect sleep; address hunger/thirst
IF RESTRAINT NECESSARY (after alternatives fail + imminent safety risk):
Provider order required, individualized, NOT PRN; in an emergency the RN may initiate and obtain the order as soon as possible (per policy)

Classify indication: NON-VIOLENT/medical-surgical (protect IV/treatment here) — renew order per policy (often daily); VIOLENT/behavioral — 1-hour face-to-face evaluation + time-limited orders (adult ≤4h, 9-17 ≤2h, <9 ≤1h) + continuous monitoring
Use the LEAST restrictive effective device (e.g., soft mitts or a single soft limb restraint); not for convenience/discipline/punishment

Safe application: secure ties to the MOVABLE BED FRAME (never side rails) with quick-release knots; 2-finger clearance; pad bony prominences; elevate HOB to protect airway; do not restrict breathing; call light within reach
Note: raising all four side rails to keep the patient in bed counts as a restraint
MONITORING + CARE WHILE RESTRAINED:
Non-violent: assess per policy (e.g., q1-2h); behavioral: CONTINUOUS monitoring
Neurovascular/circulation (color, temp, pulses, cap refill, sensation/movement), skin integrity, correct application, vital signs
At least q2h: range of motion, repositioning, offer fluids/nutrition, toileting, hygiene, reassurance (release one limb at a time as needed)
Continuously reassess need + readiness for discontinuation; DISCONTINUE at the earliest safe opportunity (trial release)
DELEGATION:
RN retains assessment, the decision to restrain, evaluation, and the decision to discontinue
UAP may perform delegated monitoring/care (vital signs, fluids, toileting, ROM, sitting/observation) with clear instruction + RN supervision
DOCUMENTATION + RIGHTS:
Document the behavior, less-restrictive measures tried + their failure, order, type/site/times applied-renewed-released, monitoring + care, patient response, injuries, and family notification
Notify/inform family per policy; preserve dignity + privacy; conduct debriefing (especially after behavioral restraint/seclusion)
MULTIDISCIPLINARY:
Hospitalist + nurse + pharmacist (deliriogenic med review) + PT/OT (mobility/deconditioning) + sitter; geriatrics/CAM-based delirium bundle
DISCHARGE PLANNING:
As delirium resolves, mobilize early, restore sleep-wake cycle, and educate family on delirium prevention/recognition for future hospitalizations

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in this agitation/restraint situation?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: Acute hyperactive delirium with behaviors endangering safety — pulling off oxygen (SpO2 89%), pulling at the IV delivering antibiotics, climbing over the rails (fall risk), and intermittent combativeness
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the safety threat + the delirium driving it. RN role: ensure immediate safety + find/treat the cause.

opts.text: The underlying cause must be sought + treated FIRST — hypoxia, pain, infection/fever, full bladder (urinary retention), medications, electrolytes/glucose, sleep deprivation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — treating the cause is the highest-yield intervention and often resolves agitation without restraint. RN role: cause-directed assessment + treatment.

opts.text: Least-restrictive alternatives should be implemented before any restraint — reorientation, glasses/hearing aid, family/sitter, low bed + alarm, frequent toileting, reduce tethers, de-escalation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — least-restrictive alternatives are required first. RN role: implement + continue nonrestrictive measures.

opts.text: If a restraint is needed, it requires an individualized provider order (NEVER PRN), the least-restrictive effective type, and the correct rules for the indication category
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — order + type + category rules govern lawful restraint use. RN role: ensure proper order + select least-restrictive device.

opts.text: Safe application + monitoring — tie to the bed frame (not side rails) with quick-release knots, 2-finger clearance, protect the airway, and monitor circulation/skin + provide ROM/toileting/fluids
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — application + monitoring prevent restraint complications. RN role: safe application + vigilant monitoring + care.

opts.text: Patient rights/dignity — restraint is a last resort, never for staff convenience/punishment, for the shortest time, with family notification, and discontinued at the earliest safe moment
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — rights + minimization + timely removal. RN role: protect rights + discontinue early.

opts.text: Avoid benzodiazepines for the agitation (they worsen delirium) unless this were alcohol/sedative withdrawal
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — benzodiazepines worsen delirium; chemical sedation is not the first answer. RN role: avoid deliriogenic sedation; treat the cause.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; relevant for any medications used. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Apply four-point limb restraints immediately because he is agitated, and write them as a PRN order so they can be reapplied whenever he gets restless
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Restraints are a last resort after least-restrictive measures, must be the least restrictive effective type (not automatically four-point), and can NEVER be ordered PRN — each episode needs an individualized order.

opts.text: Raise all four side rails and dim the room and leave him alone so he settles down without staff 'bothering' him
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Raising all four side rails to keep him in bed is itself a restraint (and a climb-over/fall hazard), and leaving an agitated delirious patient alone is unsafe + neglects monitoring. He needs treatment of the cause + observation/sitter, not isolation.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: Restraints are a last resort used only for immediate physical safety after least-restrictive measures fail — never for convenience, discipline, punishment, or as a substitute for monitoring/staffing
opts.rat: CORRECT — the foundational restraint principle. Foundation for ethical/legal practice.

opts.text: In delirium, treating the underlying cause (hypoxia, pain, infection, urinary retention, meds, electrolytes, sleep) is the highest-yield intervention and often resolves agitation without restraint; avoid benzodiazepines
opts.rat: CORRECT — cause-directed delirium management. Foundation for minimizing restraint.

opts.text: Restraint orders require an individualized provider order (never PRN); behavioral/violent restraints add a 1-hour face-to-face evaluation + time limits (adult ≤4h, 9-17 ≤2h, <9 ≤1h); non-violent orders renew per policy
opts.rat: CORRECT — order rules + category differences. Foundation for lawful restraint use.

opts.text: Safe application means the least-restrictive device, ties to the movable bed frame (not side rails) with quick-release knots, 2-finger clearance, and airway protection; four side rails up is a restraint
opts.rat: CORRECT — safe-application principles. Foundation for preventing restraint complications.

opts.text: Restraints carry serious risks (asphyxiation/death, aspiration, injury, pressure injury, neurovascular compromise, worsened delirium) and must be monitored closely and discontinued at the earliest safe time
opts.rat: CORRECT — complications + minimization principle. Foundation for monitoring + early removal.

opts.text: The RN owns the assessment, the decision to restrain, and the decision to discontinue; UAP may perform delegated monitoring/care (VS, fluids, toileting, ROM, sitting) with supervision
opts.rat: CORRECT — delegation principle. Foundation for safe task assignment.

opts.text: Once a restraint order is written, the patient should remain restrained for the full ordered duration to avoid having to reassess
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Restraints must be discontinued at the EARLIEST safe opportunity with continuous reassessment + trial release — not kept on until an order expires for staff convenience.

opts.text: A medication given specifically to sedate and control his behavior (not as standard treatment) is exempt from restraint rules because it is 'just medication'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A drug used to manage behavior/restrict movement that is not standard treatment is a CHEMICAL RESTRAINT and is subject to restraint rules + documentation.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Immediate safety + oxygenation r/t pulling off O2 (SpO2 89%) and climbing the rails — replace oxygen, ensure airway/breathing, prevent a fall, and provide constant observation
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Airway/oxygenation + fall prevention are the immediate life-safety priorities. RN role: stabilize + observe.

opts.text: Identify + treat the reversible delirium causes r/t hypoxia, full bladder, pain, infection, meds — the highest-yield way to resolve the agitation and avoid restraint
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Treating the cause resolves the behavior at its source. RN role: cause-directed assessment + treatment.

opts.text: Implement least-restrictive alternatives r/t safety + dignity — reorientation, glasses/hearing aid, family/sitter, low bed + alarm, toileting, reduce tethers, de-escalation
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Required before (and alongside) any restraint. RN role: nonrestrictive measures + sitter/family.

opts.text: If still unsafe, apply the least-restrictive restraint correctly r/t protecting essential treatment — proper (non-PRN) order, safe application to the bed frame, and close monitoring
opts.rat: #4 — When alternatives fail + imminent risk persists. RN role: least-restrictive device + proper order + safe application + monitoring.

opts.text: Protect rights + plan early discontinuation r/t dignity + complications — family notification, documentation, reassessment, trial release, and debriefing
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/foundational. Minimization + rights + timely removal. RN role: protect rights + discontinue early + document/debrief.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this situation.

opts.text: Replace the oxygen, assess airway/breathing, and provide constant observation (sitter) while urgently evaluating for reversible delirium causes
opts.rat: Addresses immediate safety + the root cause. RN role: stabilize + observe + assess.

opts.text: Treat reversible causes — relieve the full bladder, treat pain, continue antibiotics/antipyretic, review/deprescribe deliriogenic meds, correct electrolytes/glucose; avoid benzodiazepines
opts.rat: Cause-directed delirium management is the highest-yield intervention. RN role: treat the cause; avoid deliriogenic sedation.

opts.text: Implement least-restrictive measures — reorientation, glasses/hearing aid, family presence/sitter, low bed + bed alarm, frequent toileting, reduce tethers, de-escalation
opts.rat: Required first-line alternatives. RN role: nonrestrictive safety measures.

opts.text: If alternatives fail and he keeps pulling the IV delivering essential antibiotics, apply the least-restrictive device (soft mitts/single soft limb restraint) with an individualized provider order
opts.rat: Appropriate, proportionate restraint as a last resort with a proper order. RN role: least-restrictive device + order.

opts.text: Secure restraint ties to the movable bed frame with quick-release knots, ensure 2-finger clearance, elevate the head of the bed, and monitor circulation/skin with ROM/toileting/fluids
opts.rat: Safe application + monitoring prevent complications. RN role: correct application + vigilant care.

opts.text: Reassess frequently and discontinue the restraint at the earliest safe opportunity (trial release as he calms/clears), notify family, document fully, and debrief
opts.rat: Minimization + rights + documentation. RN role: early discontinuation + documentation + debrief.

opts.text: Apply four-point limb restraints first thing, before trying any alternatives, because it is faster and easier for the staff
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — restraints are a last resort (not first), must be least-restrictive (not automatically four-point), and never for staff convenience.

opts.text: Write the restraint order as PRN so night staff can reapply it whenever he becomes restless without calling the provider
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — restraint orders can NEVER be PRN; each episode requires an individualized order based on assessment.

opts.text: Tie the restraints to the side rails and raise all four rails so he cannot move, then check on him at the end of the shift
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/DANGEROUS — never tie to side rails (injury risk), four rails up is itself a restraint, and infrequent checks violate monitoring requirements (risk of asphyxiation/injury).

opts.text: Give a benzodiazepine to sedate him as the first-line way to control the delirium-related agitation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — benzodiazepines worsen delirium (except alcohol/sedative withdrawal); treat the cause + use nonpharmacologic measures first; chemical sedation to control behavior is itself a restraint.

opts.text: Keep the restraint on for the entire ordered time period regardless of his condition to avoid reassessing
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — restraints must be reassessed continually and discontinued at the earliest safe moment, not left on until an order expires.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should act. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Ensure immediate safety — replace the oxygen, assess airway/breathing, prevent a fall, and stay with him / get a sitter
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Stabilize life-safety (oxygenation + fall prevention) immediately. RN role: stabilize + observe.

opts.text: Rapidly identify and treat reversible delirium causes — bladder, pain, hypoxia, infection/fever, meds, electrolytes/glucose; avoid benzodiazepines
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Treating the cause often resolves the agitation. RN role: cause-directed treatment.

opts.text: Implement least-restrictive measures — reorientation, glasses/hearing aid, family/sitter, low bed + alarm, toileting, reduce tethers, de-escalation
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Required alternatives before restraint. RN role: nonrestrictive safety measures.

opts.text: If still endangering essential treatment/safety, apply the least-restrictive restraint with an individualized (non-PRN) order, safe application, and monitoring
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth, only if needed. RN role: proportionate restraint + proper order + safe application + monitoring.

opts.text: Reassess continually, discontinue at the earliest safe moment, notify family, document, and debrief
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/last. Minimize + protect rights + document. RN role: early discontinuation + documentation + debrief.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action taken, classify as Appropriate or Inappropriate.

opts.text: The nurse replaced the oxygen, relieved a full bladder (520 mL), treated pain and fever, reoriented him with his glasses/hearing aid, and arranged a sitter — and his agitation lessened
opts.rat: Cause-directed + least-restrictive measures that addressed the agitation at its source. Model handling.

opts.text: When he kept pulling the IV delivering essential antibiotics despite alternatives, the nurse applied soft mitts/a single soft limb restraint with an individualized (non-PRN) provider order
opts.rat: Proportionate, least-restrictive restraint as a last resort with a proper order. Correct handling.

opts.text: The restraint ties were secured to the movable bed frame with quick-release knots, 2-finger clearance was ensured, and the head of the bed was elevated to protect the airway
opts.rat: Safe application protecting circulation + airway. Model handling.

opts.text: Neurovascular/skin checks, ROM, toileting, fluids, and reassurance were provided at regular intervals, with continuous reassessment of the need
opts.rat: Required monitoring + care during restraint. Correct handling.

opts.text: The night nurse wrote the restraint as a PRN order so it could be reapplied whenever he seemed restless, without re-contacting the provider
opts.rat: ⚠️ Restraint orders can NEVER be PRN — each episode requires an individualized order. Incorrect.

opts.text: Restraints were applied first, before any least-restrictive measures, because it was faster for the busy staff
opts.rat: ⚠️ Restraints are a last resort after alternatives fail and are never for staff convenience. Incorrect.

opts.text: The restraints were tied to the side rails and all four rails were raised, and he was checked once at the end of the shift
opts.rat: ⚠️ Never tie to side rails (injury risk), four rails up is itself a restraint, and infrequent checks violate monitoring requirements (asphyxiation/injury risk). Incorrect.

opts.text: As his delirium cleared and he stopped pulling at lines, the nurse did a trial release and discontinued the restraint at the earliest safe opportunity, then documented and debriefed
opts.rat: Minimization + timely discontinuation + documentation/debrief. Model handling.

opts.text: A benzodiazepine was given as the first-line measure to sedate him into compliance
opts.rat: ⚠️ Benzodiazepines worsen delirium (except withdrawal), and using a drug to control behavior is itself a chemical restraint — not first-line. Incorrect.

opts.text: The family was notified, dignity/privacy were preserved, and the behavior, alternatives tried, order, monitoring, and response were thoroughly documented
opts.rat: Rights + family notification + complete documentation. Correct handling.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Safety and Infection Control, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
